feed.title: Wasil Zafar — Philosophy
feed.subtitle: Latest philosophy articles from Wasil Zafar

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 9: Advanced Concepts
entry.summary: Sunyata (śūnyatā, "emptiness") is the central concept of Mahayana Buddhism and one of the most subtle ideas in world philosophy. It does not mean "nothingness" or that things don't exist. It means that no thing exists with independent, intrinsic, self-sufficient nature ( svabhava ). Everything is wh

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 3: Buddhism
entry.summary: Siddhartha Gautama was born around 563 BCE in what is now Nepal, the son of a regional king. The traditional biography (almost certainly part-legend) describes a sheltered childhood in a palace where his father attempted to shield him from suffering. At twenty-nine, Siddhartha encountered the "Four

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 6: Comparative
entry.summary: The cleanest way to see how the three traditions differ is to ask the same question of each. Three questions are particularly revealing: What is the self? What does a good society look like? What is the nature of suffering and the good life? Buddhism is the most radical: there is no self at all in t

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 5: Confucianism
entry.summary: Confucius (孔子, Kongzi , "Master Kong," 551-479 BCE) was born in the small state of Lu in eastern China during the Warring States period — a time of brutal political fragmentation following the decay of the Zhou dynasty. He worked as a minor government official, tried unsuccessfully to put his ideas

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 7: East-West Dialogue
entry.summary: The deepest metaphysical contrast. Western philosophy from Aristotle onward has organized reality around substances — discrete, persisting things with essential properties. The world is fundamentally a collection of objects that have qualities, change states, enter into relations. The grammar of sub

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 2: Foundations
entry.summary: The default Western picture, inherited from Descartes, treats the mind as an inner theater observing an outer world. Eastern traditions overwhelmingly reject this division. Mind and world are aspects of a single, interdependent process. The Buddhist citta-vijñāna (mind-consciousness) does not observ

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 10: Modern Applications
entry.summary: The single most consequential modern application. Jon Kabat-Zinn , having trained at the Insight Meditation Society and earned a PhD in molecular biology, founded the Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) program at the University of Massachusetts Medical Center in 1979. He stripped Buddhist med

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 8: Practical Philosophy
entry.summary: Meditation is the foundational practice across the contemplative traditions. The instructions are simple. The practice is not. Three notes most beginners need. (1) The mind wandering is not failure; it is the data. Each return is a rep at the gym of attention. (2) Boredom, restlessness, sleepiness,

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 4: Taoism
entry.summary: 道 ( Tao , often spelled Dao ) means "way" or "path" — but in Taoist usage it names something that lies behind, beneath, and through everything that exists. The Tao is the underlying pattern, the rhythmic dynamic by which the universe unfolds. It is not a god, not a thing, not even properly an "it."

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 12: AI & Machines
entry.summary: Alan Turing 's 1950 paper "Computing Machinery and Intelligence" began with the question "Can machines think?" and immediately replaced it with one he considered better-defined: could a machine be conversationally indistinguishable from a human ? Turing's Imitation Game sets a human judge to interro

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 14: Applications
entry.summary: The field, named in 2002 by William Safire and developed by Adina Roskies , Martha Farah , and Patricia Churchland , has two halves: the ethics of neuroscience (how should we use neural technologies?) and the neuroscience of ethics (what does brain science tell us about moral judgment itself?). Prac

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 5: Consciousness
entry.summary: David Chalmers , in his 1995 paper "Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness," made the most consequential distinction in modern philosophy of mind. The "easy problems" of consciousness are easy only in comparison — they include explaining how the brain integrates information, focuses attention, re

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 9: Emotions
entry.summary: William James ("What Is an Emotion?", 1884) and Carl Lange independently proposed that emotions are perceptions of bodily changes. We do not weep because we are sad; we are sad because we weep. The bear elicits running, the running elicits the felt sensation, and that sensation just is fear. The vie

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 8: Free Will
entry.summary: The classic argument is brutally simple. Either determinism is true (every event is fully determined by prior causes plus the laws of nature) or it is false. If determinism is true, then your "choices" were fixed by the state of the universe a billion years ago. If determinism is false, then some ev

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 4: Functionalism
entry.summary: A carburetor is not defined by what it is made of (it could be brass, aluminum, or 3D-printed plastic). It is defined by what it does : mix air with fuel in the right proportions. Functionalism proposes that mental states are analogously defined: a mental state is whatever plays a certain causal-fun

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 6: Intentionality
entry.summary: The Austrian philosopher Franz Brentano , in Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint (1874), revived a medieval scholastic notion. Every mental phenomenon, he claimed, is characterized by what the scholastics called intentional inexistence — by a direction toward an object . In love there is somethi

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 13: Modern Debates
entry.summary: The classical computational view of mind treated cognition as abstract symbol manipulation that just happens to occur in a brain inside a body. The embodied cognition movement, drawing on Maurice Merleau-Ponty , Francisco Varela , and George Lakoff , rejects this. Cognition is shaped, in deep and co

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 10: Perception
entry.summary: The natural starting point: when you see a tomato, you see the tomato . Not an image, not a representation — the red, round physical object itself. This view, called direct realism or naïve realism , has obvious appeal. It is what perception feels like from the inside. We seem to be in unmediated to

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 7: Personal Identity
entry.summary: Two questions lurk in "personal identity": The diachronic question generates most of the puzzles — and most of the practical stakes (responsibility for past actions, prudence about future suffering, the meaning of survival). John Locke , in the 1694 chapter "Of Identity and Diversity" added to the s

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 3: Physicalism
entry.summary: Physicalism (also called materialism ) holds that everything that exists is physical — or, more carefully, that every entity, property, and process either is identical to a physical one or wholly depends on physical ones. Applied to mind: there are no spooky non-physical minds, souls, or mental subs

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 11: Self-Knowledge
entry.summary: For Descartes, the mind was diaphanous to itself: every mental state was, by its nature, transparently available to its owner. To have a thought was to know one had it; to feel a pain was to be aware of feeling it. There was no gap between mental fact and mental knowledge. The mind was the one domai

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 10: Applied Political Philosophy
entry.summary: Real policy decisions rarely instantiate a single value cleanly. They trade off liberty against security, equality against efficiency, present generations against future ones, individual rights against collective goods. Political philosophy provides not a recipe for resolving these trade-offs (no re

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 6: Equality, Rights & Justice
entry.summary: "Equality" is not one thing. At minimum five distinct conceptions are in use: Most political disagreements are not really about whether to value equality but about which equality and how much. A libertarian and an egalitarian can agree on moral and formal equality while disagreeing sharply on outcom

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 7: Political Ideologies
entry.summary: The dominant Western political tradition since the Enlightenment. Core commitments: the moral primacy of the individual, equal moral worth, civil and political liberties, limited government, the rule of law, religious toleration, market exchange, and (in most modern variants) representative democrac

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 2: Justice
entry.summary: Plato's Republic (c. 380 BCE) is the founding work of Western political philosophy. The dialogue begins with Socrates being challenged by the Sophist Thrasymachus, who advances the cynical thesis that "justice is the advantage of the stronger" — that what we call justice is simply whatever rules tho

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 3: Liberty & Freedom
entry.summary: Isaiah Berlin's "Two Concepts of Liberty" (1958), his Oxford inaugural lecture, is the most influential 20th-century essay on freedom. Berlin distinguished two fundamentally different things that the word "liberty" can mean. Berlin's concern, writing under the shadow of Stalinism, was that positive

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 9: Freedom in Modern Systems
entry.summary: Shoshana Zuboff 's The Age of Surveillance Capitalism (2019) gave the most influential critical-theoretical framing of the contemporary digital economy. Zuboff's thesis: a new economic logic has emerged, distinct from industrial capitalism and from anything previously theorized. The classical commod

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 8: Modern Political Philosophy
entry.summary: For most of its history, political philosophy has assumed a domestic frame: the state, its citizens, the just order among them. Globalization, mass migration, transnational corporations, and global ecological challenges have made this frame increasingly inadequate. The field of global justice , open

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 4: Power, Authority & State
entry.summary: Max Weber (1864-1920) gave the canonical sociological definitions. Power ( Macht ) is the probability that one actor can carry out his will despite resistance. Authority ( Herrschaft ) is power that those subject to it accept as legitimate — they comply not merely because they must, but because they

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 11: Research & Mastery
entry.summary: Contemporary political philosophy is methodologically pluralistic. Four broad approaches recur: Mature work increasingly combines methods. A serious treatment of any contemporary issue typically involves analytic clarification of concepts, historical attention to how those concepts came to be deploy

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 5: Social Contract
entry.summary: Covered in some detail in Part 4. Recap the structure: a brutal state of nature ("war of every man against every man"), a covenant by which each individual lays down her right of self-government in exchange for the protection of an absolute sovereign, and a resulting commonwealth in which the sovere

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 1: Orientation
entry.summary: "Eastern philosophy" is an inexact umbrella term used in the West to refer to the great philosophical traditions of Asia — most prominently those that arose in India (Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism), China (Taoism, Confucianism, Chinese Buddhism), and Japan (Zen, neo-Confucianism). In this series we fo

entry.title: Existentialism Part 11: Advanced Topics
entry.summary: This is the most consistently confused pair in popular philosophy. They are not the same. They are not even close. Nihilism (from Latin nihil , "nothing") is the metaphysical claim that life has no objective meaning, value, or purpose — and the practical conclusion that nothing therefore matters. Ni

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 4: Albert Camus — The Absurd & Revolt
entry.summary: Albert Camus (1913–1960) was born poor in French Algeria, the second son of a deaf and illiterate Spanish-Algerian mother and a father he never knew (his father was killed in the Battle of the Marne when Albert was less than a year old). He grew up in a working-class neighborhood of Algiers, was res

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 5: Core Existential Themes
entry.summary: If existentialism has a single distinguishing claim, it is this: human beings are radically free, and therefore radically responsible . Across very different metaphysics, every existentialist grapples with this twin insistence. To avoid confusion, distinguish two senses of "freedom" the existentiali

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 7: Existential Ethics
entry.summary: Dostoevsky's character Ivan Karamazov puts the worry crisply: "If God does not exist, everything is permitted." Sartre quotes the line repeatedly. The standard worry is that without a divine lawgiver, a fixed human nature, or a transcendent moral order, morality collapses into preference, will, or p

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 1: Foundations
entry.summary: Existentialism is a philosophical movement that takes the individual human being — not abstract systems, not God, not impersonal nature — as the starting point of philosophy. It asks: What does it mean to exist as a self-aware, choice-making being in a world that gives us no instruction manual? Exis

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 6: Key Thinkers
entry.summary: Nietzsche himself would have rejected the existentialist label — it did not yet exist when he wrote — but no thinker was more influential on it. Born in a Lutheran parsonage in Saxony, trained as a classical philologist, he became at 24 the youngest professor at the University of Basel, then resigne

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 2: Søren Kierkegaard — The Birth of Existential Thought
entry.summary: Søren Aabye Kierkegaard (1813–1855) lived a short, intense life almost entirely within the city limits of Copenhagen. He never held a university post, never married, never traveled much. By the time he died at 42, he had produced one of the most original bodies of philosophical and religious writing

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 9: Literature, Theater & Film
entry.summary: Long before "existentialism" was a label, the existential novel existed. Certain works of fiction stage the questions — about freedom, despair, meaning, guilt — with a vividness that argument cannot match. Sartre himself maintained that the novel was the proper genre for existential thought, because

entry.title: Existentialism Part 10: Modern Applications
entry.summary: The 21-year-old graduate today faces a labor market with a thousand specializations, lateral options, gig possibilities, and remote-vs-onsite splits. The freedom is unprecedented — and unprecedented in its capacity to paralyze. Kierkegaard's "dizziness of freedom" describes this exactly: the more po

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 8: Existential Psychology
entry.summary: The dominant 20th-century psychotherapies — Freudian psychoanalysis, behaviorism — both treated the self as a mechanism. Drives, conditioning, defense structures: explain those, and you explain the person. To clinicians who had read Kierkegaard and Heidegger, this seemed a category mistake. The pati

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 3: Jean-Paul Sartre — Radical Freedom & Bad Faith
entry.summary: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905–1980) was the public face of 20th-century existentialism. Philosopher, novelist, playwright, biographer, political activist, and, in his own description, "a man who writes" — Sartre dominated French intellectual life from the end of the Second World War until his death. He fam

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 2: Dualism
entry.summary: Although the systematic dualism of mind and matter is associated with Descartes, the basic intuition is older. Plato , in Phaedo and Republic , distinguished the immortal rational soul from the perishable body. The soul belonged to the realm of the Forms — eternal, intelligible — while the body belo

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 1: Foundations
entry.summary: Philosophy of mind is the branch of philosophy that studies the nature of the mind, mental events, mental functions, mental properties, consciousness, and their relationship to the physical body — particularly the brain. It is the rare branch of philosophy where you cannot avoid confronting the data

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 1: Foundations
entry.summary: Political philosophy is the branch of philosophy that asks normative questions about the good society, the just state, and the rightful exercise of power. It is the systematic, principled investigation of how humans should organize collective life. Every political question that matters — taxation, f

entry.title: Ethics & Moral Philosophy Series Part 6: Applied Ethics & Case Studies
entry.summary: Cookie Consent We use cookies to enhance your browsing experience. See our Privacy Policy . Apply ethical principles to real-world moral dilemmas. Explore bioethics, environmental ethics, business ethics, AI ethics, and work through contemporary case studies that challenge our moral reasoning. Appli

entry.title: Ethics & Moral Philosophy Series Part 3: Deontology & Kant
entry.summary: Cookie Consent We use cookies to enhance your browsing experience, serve personalized content, and analyze our traffic. By clicking "Accept All", you consent to our use of cookies. See our Privacy Policy for more information. Customize Settings Cookie Preferences Essential Cookies (Required) Necessa

entry.title: Ethics & Moral Philosophy Series Part 5: Moral Relativism & Nihilism
entry.summary: Cookie Consent We use cookies to enhance your browsing experience. See our Privacy Policy . Explore moral relativism, cultural relativism, and moral nihilism. Examine the profound challenges to objective morality and whether ethics can truly be universal or is inevitably culturally determined. Moral
